MCC in children: idiopathic (enlarged Peyer patches post-viral). Lead point in adults/older: Meckel, polyp, lymphoma. Dx: US target sign. Tx: Air/contrast enema (reduction) first-line. Surgery if failed/perforated.

text: A patient presents with new resting tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Parkinson Disease: TRAP = Tremor (resting, pill-rolling 4-6Hz) + Rigidity (cogwheel) + Akinesia/Bradykinesia + Postural instability. Micrographia + shuffling gait + masked facies + hypophonia. Essential tremor: action/postural, bilateral hands, improves with alcohol. Dx: Clinical. Tx: Levodopa/carbidopa (most effective), MAO-B inhibitors, DBS for advanced.

text: A patient presents with jaw claudication and temporal headache. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Giant Cell Arteritis: Jaw claudication (pathognomonic) + temporal headache + scalp tenderness + age >50 + elevated ESR/CRP. Risk: blindness (anterior ischemic optic neuropathy). TREAT IMMEDIATELY with prednisone 1mg/kg/d - DO NOT wait for biopsy. Biopsy within 2 weeks. 40% associated with polymyalgia rheumatica (shoulder/hip girdle pain).

text: A patient presents with epigastric pain relieved by food. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Duodenal Ulcer vs Gastric Ulcer: Duodenal = pain RELIEVED by food (food buffers acid) + nocturnal pain + occurs 2-3hr after meals. Gastric = pain WORSENED by food + weight loss. Both: H. pylori (test + treat) + NSAIDs. Dx: Endoscopy (biopsy gastric ulcers to exclude malignancy). Tx: PPI + H. pylori triple therapy (PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin x14d).

text: A patient presents with sudden facial droop including forehead. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Bell's Palsy vs Stroke: Forehead INVOLVED = LMN = Bell's palsy (CN VII). Forehead SPARED = UMN = stroke (bilateral cortical representation). Cause: HSV-1 reactivation of geniculate ganglion. Also: Lyme disease, sarcoidosis, parotid tumor. Tx: Prednisone (within 72hr) ± Valacyclovir (if severe). Eye protection (artificial tears + patch at night). 70-85% full recovery.

text: A patient presents with severe unilateral orbital headache with tearing. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Cluster Headache: Severe unilateral orbital/periorbital pain + autonomic features (ipsilateral tearing, ptosis, miosis, nasal congestion, rhinorrhea). Duration 15-180min, occurs in clusters (weeks), then remission. M>F (unlike migraine). Acute Tx: 100% O2 (15L/min x15min) + sumatriptan SC. Prevention: Verapamil. Migraine: aura, bilateral or unilateral, no autonomic.

text: A patient presents with progressive memory loss affecting daily activities. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Alzheimer Disease: MC dementia (60-80%). Insidious + progressive. Stages: MCI → mild (memory, language) → moderate (executive function, visuospatial) → severe (total dependence). Delirium: ACUTE fluctuating. Pseudo-dementia (depression): treat depression → improves. Dx: Clinical + MMSE/MoCA + CT/MRI (exclude reversible). Tx: Donepezil (mild-mod) + Memantine (mod-severe).

text: A patient develops hypotension, rash, and wheezing after antibiotic injection. What is the first treatment?
rationale: Anaphylaxis: Epinephrine IM (0.3-0.5mg 1:1000 in lateral thigh) = FIRST LINE, no contraindications. Mechanism: alpha-1 (vasoconstriction), beta-1 (↑HR), beta-2 (bronchodilation). Then: O2 + IV fluids + antihistamine (H1+H2) + corticosteroids (prevent biphasic). Observe 4-6hr. Discharge with EpiPen + allergy referral. Antihistamines/steroids are ADJUNCTS, never first-line.

text: A pregnant woman at 8 weeks gestation presents with vaginal bleeding and lower abdominal pain. What is the most important diagnosis to rule out?
rationale: Ectopic Pregnancy: LIFE-THREATENING. Early pregnancy + pain + bleeding = ectopic until proven otherwise. Risk: previous PID, IUD, prior tubal surgery, smoking. Dx: β-hCG + transvaginal US (no intrauterine sac when β-hCG >1500-2000 = discriminatory zone). Tx: Methotrexate (stable, unruptured) or Surgery (ruptured/unstable). Ruptured = hemodynamic instability = OR immediately.

text: A 3-week-old male infant presents with projectile non-bilious vomiting after every feeding. He appears hungry after vomiting. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Hypertrophic Pyloric Stenosis: Age 2-8 weeks + projectile NON-BILIOUS vomiting + hungry after vomiting + olive-shaped mass in epigastrium + hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis. M>F (4:1). First-born males. Dx: US (pyloric thickness >4mm, length >16mm). Tx: Pyloromyotomy (Ramstedt) AFTER correcting electrolytes. NOT a surgical emergency initially.

text: A patient presents with yellow discoloration of skin and sclera. Bilirubin is elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Jaundice Classification: Pre-hepatic (hemolysis: unconjugated bili↑, normal LFTs). Hepatic (hepatitis, cirrhosis: mixed). Post-hepatic/obstructive (gallstones, cancer: conjugated bili↑, ALP/GGT↑, pale stools, dark urine). Scleral icterus first sign. Key question: is it conjugated or unconjugated? → guides workup.

text: A 50-year-old man presents with sudden severe pain and swelling of the right big toe that woke him from sleep. Joint is red, warm, and exquisitely tender. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Gout: Acute monoarthritis of 1st MTP (podagra) + hyperuricemia + negatively birefringent needle-shaped urate crystals (monosodium urate). Triggers: alcohol, meat, diuretics, low-dose ASA. Dx: Joint aspiration (gold standard). Acute Tx: NSAIDs (first-line) or colchicine or steroids. Prevention: allopurinol (urate-lowering) if recurrent. Avoid ASA (raises urate).

text: A patient presents with productive cough for at least 3 months per year for 2 consecutive years. What is the diagnosis?
rationale: Chronic Bronchitis: Diagnostic definition: productive cough ≥3 months/year for ≥2 consecutive years (after excluding other causes). Part of COPD spectrum ("blue bloater"). Caused by: smoking #1 + air pollution. Pathology: mucus gland hypertrophy (Reid index >0.5). Tx: smoking cessation + bronchodilators + pulmonary rehab.

text: A 35-year-old man presents with sudden severe flank pain radiating to the groin, associated with nausea and hematuria. He cannot find a comfortable position. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Renal Colic (Urolithiasis): Severe colicky flank pain radiating to groin + microscopic hematuria + nausea/vomiting + patient writhing (cannot stay still, unlike peritonitis). MCC: Calcium oxalate stones (80%). Dx: CT KUB without contrast (gold standard). US for pregnancy/children. Tx: Analgesia (NSAIDs #1 + opioids) + IV fluids + alpha-blocker (tamsulosin for stones <10mm). Surgical if >10mm or obstructed.

text: A 35-year-old woman presents with fatigue, hypotension, weight loss, and hyperpigmentation of skin creases. Labs show hyponatremia and hyperkalemia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Addison Disease (Primary Adrenal Insufficiency): Hyperpigmentation (↑ACTH stimulates melanocytes) + hypotension + hyponatremia + hyperkalemia + hypoglycemia + fatigue. Causes: autoimmune (70%), TB. Dx: Morning cortisol + ACTH stimulation test (gold standard). Addisonian crisis: shock + hyponatremia + hyperkalemia → IV hydrocortisone + saline EMERGENCY. Tx: Hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone lifelong.

text: A 65-year-old man presents with bone pain, fatigue, and recurrent infections. Labs show elevated protein gap, hypercalcemia, anemia, and elevated creatinine. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Multiple Myeloma: CRAB criteria = hyperCalcemia + Renal failure + Anemia + Bone lesions (lytic 'punched-out'). Clonal plasma cells. Serum/urine protein electrophoresis: M-spike (paraprotein). Bence-Jones proteinuria. Dx: Bone marrow biopsy (>10% plasma cells). Tx: Bortezomib + dexamethasone ± stem cell transplant. Bisphosphonates for bone disease.

text: A patient is noted to have yellow deposits around both eyelids on physical exam. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Xanthelasma: Yellow-orange plaques around eyelids = lipid deposits. Associated with hyperlipidemia (especially elevated LDL). Check fasting lipid panel. Xanthomas elsewhere: tendon (Achilles → familial hypercholesterolemia), eruptive (hypertriglyceridemia), tuberous. Also seen in normal cholesterol (50%). Corneal arcus in <45yr = hyperlipidemia marker.

text: A 70-year-old postmenopausal woman suffers a wrist fracture after a minor fall. DEXA scan shows T-score of -2.7. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Osteoporosis: Fragility fracture (low-trauma) + T-score ≤-2.5 = osteoporosis. T-score -1 to -2.5 = osteopenia. Risk: postmenopausal women, smoking, steroids, low BMI. Screening: DEXA in women ≥65. Tx: Calcium + Vit D + weight-bearing exercise + bisphosphonates (alendronate, first-line). Denosumab, teriparatide for severe.

text: A 25-year-old woman presents with bloody diarrhea, urgency, and lower abdominal cramping for 6 weeks. Colonoscopy shows continuous mucosal inflammation starting from the rectum. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Ulcerative Colitis: Bloody diarrhea + mucus + tenesmus + rectal involvement (always). Continuous lesions from rectum upward (vs Crohn = skip lesions, any GI segment). Crypt abscesses on biopsy. Complications: toxic megacolon, colorectal cancer (screen after 8-10yr). Tx: 5-ASA (mesalazine) mild-mod, steroids for flares, biologics (infliximab) for severe. Proctocolectomy = curative.

text: A 24-year-old man has had two unprovoked seizures over the past year. He is otherwise healthy. What is the diagnosis?
rationale: Epilepsy: ≥2 unprovoked seizures >24hr apart OR 1 seizure with high recurrence risk = epilepsy. Types: focal (temporal lobe MCC in adults: aura + automatisms) vs generalized (tonic-clonic, absence). Dx: EEG + MRI brain. Provoked seizures (electrolytes, drugs, fever) ≠ epilepsy. Tx: Levetiracetam or valproate (generalized), carbamazepine/lamotrigine (focal). Counsel re: driving restrictions.

text: A 55-year-old man with chronic alcohol use presents with abdominal distension. On exam there is shifting dullness and a fluid wave. What is the most common underlying cause?
rationale: Ascites from Cirrhosis: Shifting dullness + fluid wave = ascites. MCC: cirrhosis (80%) → portal hypertension. SAAG ≥1.1 = portal hypertension. Tx: sodium restriction + spironolactone ± furosemide. Paracentesis if tense or diagnostic (rule out SBP: PMN >250 cells/μL). SBP = spontaneous bacterial peritonitis → IV cefotaxime + albumin.

text: A 19-year-old swimmer presents with severe ear pain worsened by pulling the tragus, and purulent discharge. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Otitis Externa (Swimmer's Ear): External canal inflammation + tragus tenderness + discharge. MCC: Pseudomonas aeruginosa + S. aureus (moisture creates environment). Tx: Topical cipro/dexamethasone drops + keep dry. Otitis media = pain + fever + TM bulging/opacification (no tragus tenderness). Malignant OE (in diabetics/immunocompromised) = bone invasion → IV antibiotics.

text: A 12-year-old child presents with sudden onset fever, sore throat, and exudative tonsils. Centor criteria are met. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Strep Pharyngitis: Centor criteria: exudate + tender anterior cervical LAD + fever + NO cough (each scores 1pt). Score ≥3 → treat or rapid strep test first. MCC: GAS (Group A Strep). Tx: Penicillin V x10d (first-line) or amoxicillin. Penicillin-allergic: azithromycin. Untreated → rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis. Viral pharyngitis (EBV/adenovirus) = cough + coryza usually present.

text: A 55-year-old man presents with muscle weakness that is WORSE at rest and improves with repeated use. He also has autonomic dysfunction. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome: Proximal weakness IMPROVES with repeated use (opposite of MG). Reduced ACh release from presynaptic terminal. Associated with SCLC (small cell lung cancer). Dx: Anti-VGCC antibodies + EMG (incremental response). Screen for occult malignancy. Tx: Treat underlying cancer + 3,4-DAP + IVIG. MG = worse with use, anti-AChR Ab, decremental EMG.

text: A 65-year-old woman presents with sudden electric shock-like pain on one side of her face triggered by chewing or touching. Pain lasts seconds. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Trigeminal Neuralgia (Tic Douloureux): Brief severe unilateral facial pain (electric/stabbing) triggered by light touch, chewing, talking. Distribution: CN V (V2/V3 most common). MCC in elderly: vascular compression of CN V. In young: rule out MS. Dx: MRI brain. Tx: Carbamazepine (first-line). Surgical: MVD (microvascular decompression) for refractory.

text: A 35-year-old woman presents with difficulty swallowing BOTH solids and liquids from the very beginning. She also has regurgitation of undigested food. Barium swallow shows bird-beak narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Achalasia: Failure of LES relaxation + absent esophageal peristalsis. Dysphagia to BOTH solids AND liquids from onset (vs cancer = solids first then liquids). Barium: bird-beak/rat-tail tapering. Dilated esophagus. Dx: Manometry (gold standard: absent peristalsis + incomplete LES relaxation). CXR: dilated esophagus with air-fluid level. Tx: Pneumatic dilation or Heller myotomy.

text: A 28-year-old woman presents with chronic diarrhea, bloating, and weight loss after eating wheat, rye, or barley. Small bowel biopsy shows villous atrophy. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Celiac Disease: Gluten-sensitive enteropathy → villous atrophy → malabsorption. Symptoms: diarrhea, steatorrhea, weight loss, bloating, iron deficiency anemia. Extraintestinal: dermatitis herpetiformis, osteoporosis, infertility. Dx: Anti-tTG IgA (most sensitive/specific) + duodenal biopsy (gold standard). Tx: Strict lifelong gluten-free diet. Risk if untreated: T-cell lymphoma.

text: A patient with schizophrenia started on haloperidol develops fever (40°C), severe muscle rigidity, diaphoresis, and altered consciousness. CK is markedly elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS): FEVER + RIGIDITY + altered consciousness + autonomic instability after ANTIPSYCHOTICS. Markedly elevated CK → rhabdomyolysis → renal failure. Onset: days-weeks after starting/increasing antipsychotics. Tx: STOP antipsychotic immediately + supportive care + dantrolene (muscle relaxant) + bromocriptine (dopamine agonist) + ICU. vs Serotonin syndrome = clonus/hyperreflexia (not rigidity) + SSRIs.

text: A patient on an SSRI takes an extra dose of another serotonergic drug and develops agitation, tremor, clonus, hyperreflexia, and fever. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Serotonin Syndrome: Triad = Mental status change + Autonomic instability + Neuromuscular abnormalities (CLONUS = key differentiator). Caused by: SSRI + MAOI, SSRI + tramadol, SSRI + linezolid. Onset: hours (rapid). Hunter criteria for Dx. Tx: Stop offending agent + benzodiazepines + cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist) + supportive. NMS = rigidity (not clonus) + antipsychotics + days-weeks onset.

text: A 22-year-old woman using tampons during her period develops sudden high fever, diffuse rash resembling sunburn, hypotension, and multi-organ involvement. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Toxic Shock Syndrome (TSS): Staphylococcal TSS = tampon use + high fever + diffuse erythroderma (sunburn rash) + hypotension + desquamation (especially palms/soles 1-2wks later) + multi-organ involvement. Cause: TSST-1 superantigen from S. aureus. Streptococcal TSS = GAS, more commonly associated with soft tissue infection/necrotizing fasciitis. Tx: Remove source + IV fluids + clindamycin (inhibits toxin) + beta-lactam.

text: A 45-year-old woman presents with dry eyes (xerophthalmia) and dry mouth (xerostomia). She also has difficulty swallowing and parotid gland enlargement. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Sjögren Syndrome: Sicca complex = dry eyes + dry mouth. Primary (alone) or secondary (with RA, SLE). F>M (9:1). Abs: Anti-Ro/SSA (most sensitive) + Anti-La/SSB. Complications: lymphoma (MALT), interstitial nephritis, peripheral neuropathy. Schirmer test (<5mm in 5min = abnormal). Tx: Artificial tears/saliva + pilocarpine + hydroxychloroquine for systemic.

text: A 5-year-old boy presents with progressive proximal muscle weakness, difficulty climbing stairs, and pseudohypertrophy of calves. CK is very elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy: X-linked recessive + dystrophin gene mutation. Males. Onset 2-5yr: Gowers' sign (climbing up legs to stand) + pseudohypertrophy calves (fat/fibrotic replacement) + elevated CK (>10x). Cardiomyopathy (always screen). Dx: Genetic testing + muscle biopsy (absent dystrophin). Tx: Prednisone (slows progression) + cardiac/respiratory support. Wheelchair-bound by adolescence.

text: A 70-year-old man with AF presents with sudden severe diffuse abdominal pain that seems out of proportion to a relatively soft abdomen. He has a history of heart disease. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Acute Mesenteric Ischemia: Pain OUT OF PROPORTION to physical exam = classic hallmark. Embolic (AF #1) or thrombotic. Gut ischemia → infarction → necrosis. Early: soft abdomen. Late: peritonitis + bloody diarrhea. Dx: CT angiography (gold standard). Tx: Emergent laparotomy + revascularization ± bowel resection + anticoagulation. High mortality (60-80%). Don't delay for labs - clinical diagnosis!

text: A 25-year-old farmer presents with trismus (lockjaw), painful muscle spasms, and opisthotonos after a puncture wound 2 weeks ago. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Tetanus: Clostridium tetani toxin (tetanospasmin) → blocks inhibitory neurotransmitters → spastic paralysis. Trismus (MC presenting sx) + risus sardonicus + opisthotonos. History of wound. Tx: Tetanus immune globulin (TIG) + wound debridement + benzos (muscle spasms) + ICU + metronidazole. Prevention: Tdap booster every 10yr. Botulism = DESCENDING paralysis + no fever + from toxin in food.

text: A family develops diplopia, dysarthria, and descending weakness hours after eating home-canned food. There is no fever. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Botulism: Clostridium botulinum toxin → blocks ACh release at NMJ → DESCENDING flaccid paralysis. Sources: home-canned food, honey (infant botulism), wound. Cranial nerves first (diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia) then descending. AFEBRILE (key distinction). Dx: Clinical + EMG + toxin assay. Tx: Antitoxin (early) + supportive + ventilatory support. Guillain-Barré = ASCENDING + post-infectious + CSF albuminocytologic dissociation.

text: A 28-year-old woman develops progressive ascending weakness and areflexia 2 weeks after a GI illness. CSF shows elevated protein with normal cell count. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Guillain-Barré Syndrome (AIDP): Ascending flaccid weakness + areflexia + post-infectious (Campylobacter, CMV, EBV). CSF: albuminocytologic dissociation (↑protein, normal WBC). EMG: demyelinating. Risk: respiratory failure (monitor FVC). Tx: IVIG or plasmapheresis (equally effective). NO steroids. 85% full recovery. Anti-ganglioside Ab (anti-GQ1b = Miller Fisher variant: ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + areflexia).

text: A 55-year-old woman presents with kidney stones, constipation, fatigue, and depression. Labs show hypercalcemia and elevated PTH. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Primary Hyperparathyroidism: Bones, Stones, Groans (abdominal), Psychic moans = hypercalcemia symptoms. Elevated PTH + hypercalcemia = primary HPT. MCC: parathyroid adenoma (80%). Dx: PTH + serum calcium. Imaging: sestamibi scan to localize. Tx: Parathyroidectomy if symptomatic or age <50/Ca >0.25 above normal/osteoporosis/renal disease. Cinacalcet for inoperable. FHH (familial): high Ca + low/normal PTH + low urine Ca.

text: A 2-year-old child from a developing country presents with bowed legs, widened wrists, and soft skull bones. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Rickets: Vitamin D deficiency in children → impaired bone mineralization. Signs: bowing of legs, rachitic rosary (costochondral beading), craniotabes (soft skull), widened metaphyses, Harrison groove. Labs: low vitamin D, low Ca/P, elevated ALP, secondary ↑PTH. Dx: X-ray (cupping/fraying of metaphyses). Tx: Vitamin D replacement + calcium + sun exposure. Osteomalacia = adult equivalent.

text: A patient with diabetes insipidus presents with severe thirst, confusion, and dry mucous membranes. Serum sodium is 158 mmol/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Hypernatremia: Na >145 = water deficit (usually). Symptoms: thirst, confusion, seizures (severe). Causes: DI (central or nephrogenic) + inadequate water intake. Dx: Urine osmolality + response to desmopressin. Central DI: concentrated urine after desmopressin. Nephrogenic DI: no response. Tx: Free water replacement (D5W or PO water). Correct SLOWLY (<0.5 mmol/L/hr) to avoid cerebral edema.

text: A patient post-thyroidectomy presents with perioral tingling, muscle cramps, and a positive Chvostek sign. Calcium is 1.8 mmol/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Hypocalcemia: Post-thyroidectomy/parathyroidectomy = MCC of acute hypocalcemia (parathyroid glands damaged). Symptoms: perioral tingling + tetany + muscle cramps + seizures. Chvostek sign (facial twitch) + Trousseau sign (carpal spasm with BP cuff). Severe: prolonged QT → arrhythmia. Tx: IV calcium gluconate (acute) + oral calcium + vitamin D (chronic). Also: hypomagnesemia → hypocalcemia (give Mg first).

text: A 70-year-old man with hypertension presents with palpitations. ECG shows no discrete P waves and irregularly irregular QRS complexes. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Atrial Fibrillation: Irregularly irregular rhythm + absent P waves + irregular QRS = AF. MCC arrhythmia requiring hospitalization. Risk: hypertension, valvular disease, heart failure, hyperthyroidism, alcohol. Stroke risk: CHA₂DS₂-VASc score guides anticoagulation. Rate control: beta-blockers/CCBs. Rhythm control: cardioversion + antiarrhythmics. Anticoagulation: DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) preferred.

text: A 55-year-old man with hypertension presents with predictable chest pressure on exertion that is completely relieved by rest within 5 minutes. ECG is normal at rest. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Stable Angina: Predictable exertional chest pain/pressure + relieved by rest in <5min or nitroglycerin. Fixed coronary stenosis (>70%). ECG: normal at rest, ST depression on exercise. Dx: Stress test (exercise ECG, nuclear, echo). Tx: Lifestyle + ASA + beta-blocker + statin + nitroglycerin PRN. Unstable angina = rest pain/crescendo pattern → ACS protocol.

text: A 65-year-old smoker presents with hoarseness lasting more than 6 weeks. He also drinks alcohol regularly. What is the most concerning diagnosis?
rationale: Laryngeal Cancer: Persistent hoarseness >3 weeks in smoker/drinker = laryngeal cancer until proven otherwise. Risk: smoking + alcohol (synergistic). MC type: SCC. Hoarseness = early sign for glottic tumors (good prognosis) vs supraglottic (late symptoms, poor prognosis). Dx: Laryngoscopy + biopsy. Tx: Radiation (early) or surgery ± chemoradiation (advanced). Voice-sparing whenever possible.

text: A 70-year-old immunocompromised patient presents with severe unilateral burning pain followed by a vesicular rash in a band-like distribution on the trunk. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Herpes Zoster (Shingles): VZV reactivation → dermatomal vesicular rash + pain. Preceded by prodromal pain/itching. Affects elderly/immunocompromised. Complications: PHN (post-herpetic neuralgia) = most common; Ramsay Hunt (CN VII: facial palsy + ear vesicles + hearing loss); Ophthalmicus (CN V1: corneal involvement). Tx: Antivirals within 72hr (valacyclovir/acyclovir) + pain management. Prevention: Shingrix vaccine.

text: A 5-year-old child presents with honey-colored crusted lesions around the nose and mouth. They started as small blisters that ruptured. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Impetigo: Superficial skin infection + honey-colored crusts = hallmark. Cause: S. aureus (bullous) or GAS (non-bullous, MC). Highly contagious. Children > adults. Tx: Topical mupirocin (localized) or oral cephalexin/cloxacillin (extensive). Bullous impetigo = S. aureus toxin → flaccid bullae. Complication: GAS → post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (NOT rheumatic fever from skin infection).

text: A 35-year-old patient presents with well-demarcated erythematous plaques with thick silvery scales on the elbows and scalp. The lesions are pruritic. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Psoriasis: Chronic autoimmune skin disease. Well-demarcated silver-scaled plaques on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees, scalp, sacrum). Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding on scale removal). Nail pitting + onycholysis. Associations: psoriatic arthritis (asymmetric oligoarthritis). Tx: Topical steroids + vitamin D analogs (calcipotriol) + biologics (anti-TNF, anti-IL-17) for moderate-severe.

text: A 50-year-old diabetic presents with thickened, yellow, crumbling toenails with nail separation from the bed. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Onychomycosis: Fungal nail infection. Yellow/white/brown + thick + crumbling nails + onycholysis. MCC: Trichophyton rubrum (dermatophyte). Risk: diabetes, peripheral vascular disease, elderly, humid environment. Dx: KOH prep + fungal culture or PCR. Tx: Oral terbinafine (most effective, 3mo toes) or itraconazole. Topical efinaconazole/ciclopirox for mild. Monitor LFTs with oral antifungals.

text: A 25-year-old woman presents with intense pruritus worse at night, affecting the finger webs, wrists, and genitals. Her partner has similar symptoms. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Scabies: Sarcoptes scabiei mite. Intense pruritus worse at night + interdigital/wrist/genital burrows + household contacts affected. Dx: Mineral oil scraping + microscopy (mite/eggs/feces). Tx: Topical permethrin 5% (entire body, leave 8-12hr, repeat in 1wk) OR oral ivermectin. Treat ALL household contacts simultaneously. Wash all clothing/bedding in hot water. Norwegian (crusted) scabies = immunocompromised + highly infectious.

text: A 45-year-old obese man presents with daytime somnolence, morning headaches, and his wife reports loud snoring with witnessed apneas. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Obstructive Sleep Apnea: Loud snoring + witnessed apneas + excessive daytime sleepiness (Epworth Sleepiness Scale) + morning headaches (hypercapnia). Risk: obesity, male, large neck, retrognathia. Dx: Polysomnography (PSG = gold standard) or home sleep test (if uncomplicated). Tx: CPAP (first-line) + weight loss + positional therapy. Untreated: HTN, arrhythmias, MI, stroke, MVA risk.

text: A 3-year-old girl presents with recurrent UTIs and left-sided flank pain after urination. Renal ultrasound shows left hydronephrosis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Vesicoureteral Reflux (VUR): Retrograde urine flow from bladder to ureter/kidney during voiding. MCC of recurrent UTI in children. Risk: renal scarring → CKD/hypertension. Dx: Voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG = gold standard). Grade I-V (I = ureter only, V = severe dilation). Tx: Prophylactic antibiotics (TMP-SMX) for low grades. Surgical correction (ureteral reimplantation) for high grades or breakthrough infections.

text: A 60-year-old man presents with severe crushing chest pain, diaphoresis, and nausea for 45 minutes. ECG shows ST elevation in leads V1-V4. What is the diagnosis and most appropriate next step?
rationale: STEMI: ST elevation in ≥2 contiguous leads + typical symptoms = STEMI. V1-V4 = anterior STEMI (LAD territory). Time-sensitive EMERGENCY. Door-to-balloon goal: <90min. Immediate: Dual antiplatelet (ASA + P2Y12 inhibitor) + anticoagulation + activate cath lab for primary PCI. If PCI not available within 120min → thrombolysis. ECG: STEMI vs LBBB (new LBBB = treat as STEMI). Sgarbossa criteria for LBBB.

text: A 65-year-old man presents with sudden onset chest pain radiating to his left arm. He appears diaphoretic and nauseated. His blood pressure is 90/60 mmHg. What is the most appropriate initial investigation?
rationale: ECG must be obtained within 10 minutes in suspected MI to detect ST elevation. Troponin confirms later but ECG guides immediate management.

text: A 54-year-old man presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back. He reports heavy alcohol use. Serum amylase is mildly elevated. Which test is most specific for confirming the diagnosis?
rationale: Lipase is more specific and sensitive for acute pancreatitis than amylase. Amylase can be elevated in many other conditions.

text: A 70-year-old woman presents with sudden weakness of the right arm and difficulty speaking that began one hour ago. What is the most appropriate initial investigation?
rationale: CT head is required first to exclude intracranial hemorrhage before considering thrombolysis. MRI is more sensitive but takes longer.

text: A 45-year-old patient with COPD receives high-flow oxygen in the emergency department. Shortly after, he becomes drowsy and his PaCO₂ rises. What is the most appropriate management?
rationale: COPD patients should maintain O₂ saturation 88-92%. High-flow oxygen suppresses hypoxic drive → CO₂ retention → hypercapnic respiratory failure.

text: A 4-month-old infant presents with cough, wheezing and rhinorrhea. Examination shows respiratory distress and diffuse crackles. What is the most likely cause?
rationale: RSV (Respiratory Syncytial Virus) is the most common cause of bronchiolitis in infants under 2 years. Treatment is supportive care.

text: A 30-year-old woman presents with palpitations, weight loss and heat intolerance. Examination shows diffuse goiter and exophthalmos. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Graves disease is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism. Exophthalmos (proptosis) is pathognomonic for Graves disease specifically.

text: A 50-year-old man presents with progressive dysphagia and weight loss. He reports difficulty swallowing solids first and then liquids. Which investigation is most appropriate?
rationale: Endoscopy with biopsy is the gold standard to confirm esophageal or gastric malignancy. Progressive solid-then-liquid dysphagia = mechanical obstruction.

text: A child presents with fever, drooling and inspiratory stridor. He is sitting leaning forward (tripod position) and appears anxious. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Epiglottitis: drooling + tripod position + stridor + high fever. Do NOT examine throat. Secure airway immediately + IV antibiotics.

text: A 22-year-old man presents with fever, headache and neck stiffness. Lumbar puncture shows increased neutrophils, decreased glucose, and increased protein. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Bacterial meningitis: neutrophilic CSF + low glucose + high protein. Viral = lymphocytic CSF + normal glucose. Treat immediately with IV antibiotics.

text: A 32-year-old woman with known bipolar disorder presents with elevated mood, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, and pressured speech for the past week. Which medication is first-line therapy?
rationale: Lithium is the first-line mood stabilizer for bipolar disorder. SSRIs alone can trigger mania. Monitor lithium levels, renal function and thyroid.

text: A 40-year-old woman presents with severe headache described as the worst headache of her life. It started suddenly while exercising. Neurological exam is normal. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Thunderclap headache = worst headache of life = subarachnoid hemorrhage until proven otherwise. Dx: CT head → if negative → LP for xanthochromia.

text: A 45-year-old patient with schizophrenia has been on haloperidol for 5 years. He now develops involuntary repetitive tongue movements and lip smacking. Diagnosis?
rationale: Tardive dyskinesia: chronic antipsychotic use → orofacial involuntary movements. Treatment: stop drug, switch to clozapine or use VMAT2 inhibitors.

text: A 68-year-old man with atrial fibrillation has a CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of 3. He has no contraindications to anticoagulation. What medication should be prescribed for stroke prevention?
rationale: CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 in men = anticoagulation indicated. DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) preferred over warfarin. Aspirin alone insufficient for stroke prevention in AF.

text: A pregnant woman at 32 weeks presents with blood pressure 160/110 mmHg, proteinuria 2+ and bilateral leg edema. She has a severe headache. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Preeclampsia: hypertension + proteinuria after 20 weeks. Severe features include BP >160/110 and headache. Eclampsia = preeclampsia + seizures.

text: A woman with severe preeclampsia at 34 weeks is admitted. Blood pressure is 170/115 mmHg. What medication is most appropriate to prevent seizures?
rationale: Magnesium sulfate is the drug of choice for seizure prophylaxis AND treatment in preeclampsia/eclampsia. Monitor for toxicity: loss of reflexes, respiratory depression.

text: A 60-year-old man presents with sudden crushing chest pain radiating to the left arm. He appears diaphoretic. What is the most appropriate first investigation?
rationale: ECG must be obtained within 10 minutes in suspected MI to detect ST elevation and guide reperfusion therapy.

text: Which cardiac biomarker is most specific for myocardial infarction?
rationale: Troponin is the most sensitive and specific marker for myocardial injury. Rises at 3-4h, peaks at 24h, stays elevated 10 days.

text: A patient with acute MI should receive which medication immediately upon arrival?
rationale: Aspirin reduces mortality by inhibiting platelet aggregation. Give 325mg chewed immediately in suspected MI.

text: A patient presents with irregularly irregular pulse, fatigue and palpitations. ECG shows no P waves. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: AF: irregularly irregular rhythm + absent P waves on ECG. Most common sustained arrhythmia. Causes: hypertension, valvular disease, hyperthyroidism.

text: What is the most important complication of atrial fibrillation?
rationale: AF causes stasis in left atrium → thrombus → embolic stroke. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score guides anticoagulation.

text: Which medication prevents stroke in atrial fibrillation?
rationale: Anticoagulation (DOAC preferred over warfarin) prevents systemic embolism in AF. Aspirin alone is insufficient.

text: A systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur radiating to the carotids is most characteristic of which condition?
rationale: Aortic stenosis: harsh systolic murmur at RUSB → carotids. Classic triad: Syncope, Angina, Dyspnea (SAD).

text: A patient presents with hypotension, distended neck veins and muffled heart sounds after a stab wound to the chest. Diagnosis?
rationale: Beck triad = Hypotension + JVD + Muffled heart sounds = Cardiac tamponade. Treatment: pericardiocentesis.

text: Most common cause of heart failure in developed countries?
rationale: CAD (ischemic cardiomyopathy) is #1 cause of heart failure. Hypertension is #2.

text: A 72-year-old with heart failure presents with acute dyspnea and bilateral crackles. Most appropriate first treatment?
rationale: Acute pulmonary edema: IV loop diuretics (furosemide) reduce preload. Also give supplemental O₂ and position upright.

text: Which medication improves survival in chronic systolic heart failure?
rationale: ACE inhibitors (enalapril, ramipril) reduce mortality by preventing cardiac remodeling. Also: beta blockers and spironolactone improve survival.

text: ST elevation in leads II, III and aVF. Which artery is most likely occluded?
rationale: Inferior STEMI (II, III, aVF) = RCA territory. Anterior (V1-V4) = LAD. Lateral (I, aVL, V5-V6) = LCx.

text: A 65-year-old smoker has chronic productive cough for 3 months per year for 2 consecutive years. Diagnosis?
rationale: Chronic bronchitis definition: productive cough ≥3 months/year for ≥2 consecutive years. Part of COPD spectrum.

text: Target oxygen saturation in COPD patients receiving supplemental oxygen?
rationale: COPD target: 88-92%. Higher O₂ suppresses hypoxic drive → CO₂ retention → hypercapnic respiratory failure.

text: Most common cause of bronchiolitis in infants under 2 years?
rationale: RSV = #1 cause of bronchiolitis. Treatment is supportive (hydration, O₂). Palivizumab for high-risk infants.

text: Most common cause of community-acquired pneumonia?
rationale: S. pneumoniae = #1 CAP. Atypical (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella) in young adults. Klebsiella in alcoholics.

text: Best diagnostic test for pulmonary embolism in a hemodynamically stable patient?
rationale: CTPA is gold standard for PE. D-dimer used to RULE OUT PE in low probability. ECG may show S1Q3T3.

text: A patient presents with sudden dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain after a long flight. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Classic PE scenario: immobility (long flight/surgery) + sudden pleuritic chest pain + dyspnea. Risk factors: Virchow triad.

text: Initial anticoagulant treatment of pulmonary embolism?
rationale: Start LMWH (or UFH) immediately. Thrombolysis reserved for massive PE with hemodynamic instability. Warfarin started concurrently but takes days to reach therapeutic level.

text: A patient presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea and vomiting. Serum lipase is 3x upper limit. Diagnosis?
rationale: Acute pancreatitis: epigastric pain radiating to back + elevated lipase (>3x ULN). Atlanta criteria for severity.

text: Most common cause of acute pancreatitis?
rationale: Gallstones #1 (40-70%), Alcohol #2 (30%). Remember: GET SMASHED mnemonic for causes.

text: Most specific laboratory test for pancreatitis?
rationale: Lipase is more specific than amylase. Amylase can be elevated in bowel obstruction, salivary gland disease, renal failure.

text: A patient presents with RUQ pain, fever and jaundice after a fatty meal. Diagnosis?
rationale: Charcot triad: RUQ pain + fever + jaundice = cholangitis. RUQ pain + fever without jaundice = cholecystitis (Murphy sign positive).

text: Most common cause of upper GI bleeding?
rationale: PUD = #1 cause of upper GI bleeding. Varices in cirrhotic patients. All present with hematemesis or melena.

text: Gold standard test for suspected gastric cancer?
rationale: Upper endoscopy + biopsy confirms gastric cancer. H. pylori is major risk factor. Progressive solid dysphagia + weight loss = malignancy until proven otherwise.

text: First imaging test for suspected acute stroke?
rationale: CT head first to exclude hemorrhagic stroke before tPA. MRI more sensitive but slower. Time is brain.

text: Time window for IV tPA thrombolysis in ischemic stroke?
rationale: IV tPA: within 4.5 hours of onset. Mechanical thrombectomy up to 24h for large vessel occlusion. Contraindicated in hemorrhagic stroke.

text: Most common cause of dementia in the elderly?
rationale: Alzheimer = #1 dementia (60-70%). Vascular dementia #2 (stepwise progression). Lewy body = dementia + visual hallucinations + Parkinsonism.

text: Classic presenting sign of Parkinson disease?
rationale: TRAP: Tremor (resting, pill-rolling), Rigidity (cogwheel), Akinesia/bradykinesia, Postural instability. Tx: Levodopa.

text: Most common cause of subarachnoid hemorrhage?
rationale: Spontaneous SAH = ruptured saccular (berry) aneurysm 80%. Thunderclap headache = worst headache of life. CT → LP if CT negative.

text: A newborn develops jaundice on day 3 of life. He is breastfed and otherwise healthy. Most likely cause?
rationale: Physiologic jaundice peaks day 3-5 in term infants (day 2-4 formula-fed). Caused by immature bilirubin conjugation. Treat with phototherapy if approaching exchange threshold.

text: Most common congenital heart defect?
rationale: VSD is #1 congenital heart defect (25-30%). Most small VSDs close spontaneously. Large VSD: left-to-right shunt → pulmonary hypertension → Eisenmenger.

text: Child with 5-day fever, bilateral conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, swollen hands/feet and cervical lymphadenopathy. Diagnosis?
rationale: Kawasaki: CRASH criteria = Conjunctivitis, Rash, Adenopathy, Strawberry tongue/lip changes, Hand/foot swelling. Dx: fever >5 days + 4/5 criteria. Tx: IVIG.

text: Treatment for Kawasaki disease?
rationale: IVIG (2g/kg single dose) reduces coronary artery aneurysm risk. High-dose aspirin for anti-inflammatory, then low-dose for antiplatelet.

text: First-line treatment for major depressive disorder?
rationale: SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine, escitalopram) = first-line for MDD. Safe, effective, minimal side effects. Start low, titrate. Allow 4-6 weeks for full effect.

text: Mood stabilizer of choice for bipolar disorder?
rationale: Lithium = first-line mood stabilizer. Monitor levels (narrow TI), renal function, thyroid. Toxicity: tremor, ataxia, confusion, seizures.

text: OCD treatment combination?
rationale: OCD: SSRIs (high dose) + CBT with Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP). CBT alone can be effective. SSRIs alone: 40-60% response.

text: Most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage?
rationale: Uterine atony = #1 PPH cause (80%). 4 Ts: Tone (atony), Trauma, Tissue (retained placenta), Thrombin (coagulopathy). Tx: oxytocin first-line.

text: Drug of choice to prevent seizures in severe preeclampsia?
rationale: MgSO4 is drug of choice for seizure prevention (prophylaxis) AND treatment (eclampsia). Monitor for toxicity: loss of DTRs → respiratory depression.

text: Treatment for stable unruptured ectopic pregnancy?
rationale: Methotrexate (IM) for stable, unruptured ectopic with hCG <5000 and no cardiac activity. Surgery for rupture, instability, or failed MTX.

text: Gold standard screening test for colon cancer?

text: Screening test for osteoporosis?

text: Vaccine that prevents cervical cancer?

text: A 45-year-old patient on haloperidol for 7 years develops involuntary lip smacking and repetitive tongue movements. Diagnosis?

text: A patient has fever, neck stiffness and photophobia. CSF shows neutrophils, low glucose, high protein. Most appropriate immediate treatment?
